perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware that the Receiving Party and its Representatives should consider securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit warning about insider trading prohibitions creates a legal risk, as the agreement no longer clearly reminds the Receiving Party and its Representatives of these critical regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the acknowledgment of insider trading laws, contradicting the intent of Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit deceptive practices in securities trading. The original text explicitly advises about the prohibitions, while the modified text only suggests consideration.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibits insider trading, aligning with the intent of Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 as described in the Law Explanation. The modified text weakens this by only suggesting consideration of securities laws, thereby omitting the explicit warning. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, states it is unlawful to 'employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud' or 'omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made...not misleading...in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.' The omission of the explicit insider trading warning weakens the protection against such practices, establishing a legal contradiction by potentially misleading the receiving party about their obligations and removing essential information regarding insider trading prohibitions, which is a legally mandated investor/consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws. Each of the parties hereto: (a) irrevocably and unconditionally consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of New York for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement; (b) agrees that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to the address set forth at the end of this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against such party; (c) irrevocably and unconditionally waives any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement in any state or federal court located in the State of New York; and (d) irrevocably and unconditionally waives the right to plead or claim, and irrevocably and unconditionally agrees not to plead or claim, that any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement that is brought in any state or federal court located in the State of New York has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York. Each of the parties hereto: (a) consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts; (b) agrees that service of process shall be effective; (c) waives any objection to the laying of venue of any action; and (d) waives the right to plead or claim that any action has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'irrevocably and unconditionally' weakens the enforceability of the jurisdiction and venue provisions. New York law favors explicit and unambiguous waivers. Removing these words introduces ambiguity, making it easier for a party to challenge jurisdiction or venue later.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 301
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text provides an explicit waiver of objections to jurisdiction, as is favored by NY law, whereas the changed text's lack of explicitness regarding irrevocability weakens that waiver. NY CPLR 301 addresses jurisdiction over persons, and NY courts expect clear and unambiguous consent to jurisdiction.

Jurisdiction over persons, property or status. A court may exercise such jurisdiction over persons, property, or status as might have been exercised heretofore.
perturbation.justification: The removal of "irrevocably and unconditionally" weakens the jurisdiction and venue provisions, potentially contradicting New York law's requirement for clear and unambiguous consent to jurisdiction. The original text provides an explicit waiver of objections to jurisdiction, which is favored by New York law. The altered text's lack of explicitness regarding irrevocability undermines that waiver. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state 'irrevocably and unconditionally,' the Law Explanation implies that New York courts expect clear and unambiguous consent to jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines that a party has breached this Agreement, then such party shall be liable and pay to the non-breaching party the legal fees and expenses such non-breaching party has incurred in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own legal fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that awards legal fees to the non-breaching party introduces a legal risk. New York law permits fee-shifting clauses but does not automatically award fees. By removing this, the contract deviates from the potentially more favorable position the original clause provided under New York law and common practice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 5001
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause allows for the non-breaching party to recover legal fees, which aligns with the principles of making the injured party whole. By stating that each party shall bear its own legal fees, the clause now implies that the breaching party is not responsible for paying the other party's legal fees, despite being responsible for the breach. NY CPLR 5001 discusses the recovery of interest in breach of contract cases, and a fee-shifting clause would shift more costs to the breaching party.

Interest to verdict, report or decision. (a) Actions in which recoverable. Interest shall be recovered upon a sum awarded because of a breach of performance of a contract, or because of an act or omission depriving or otherwise interfering with title to, or possession or enjoyment of, property, except that in an action of an equitable nature, interest and the rate and date from which it shall be computed shall be in the court's discretion. (b) Date from which computed. Interest shall be computed from the earliest ascertainable date the cause of action existed, except that interest upon damages incurred thereafter shall be computed from the date incurred. Where such damages were incurred at various times, interest shall be computed upon each item from the date it was incurred or upon all of the damages from a single reasonable intermediate date. (c) Specifying date; computing interest. The date from which interest is to be computed shall be specified in the verdict, report or decision. If a jury is discharged without specifying the date, the court upon motion shall fix the date, except that where the date is certain and not in dispute, the date may be fixed by the clerk of the court upon affidavit. The amount of interest shall be computed by the clerk of the court, to the date the verdict was rendered or the report or decision was made, and included in the total sum awarded.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text provided that a breaching party would be liable for the non-breaching party's legal fees. The modified contract states that each party shall bear its own legal fees and expenses, regardless of breach. This contradicts the principle of making the injured party whole, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, and deviates from the potentially more favorable position the original clause provided under New York law and common practice, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. NY CPLR 5001(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that "Interest shall be recovered upon a sum awarded because of a breach of performance of a contract..." A fee-shifting clause would shift more costs to the breaching party, and the modification removes this possibility.

perturbation.original_text: Without the prior written consent of the Company or except as may be required by applicable law or regulation, the Receiving Party and its Representatives shall not disclose to any person that any discussions or negotiations are taking place between the parties concerning a possible Transaction, including the content, timing and status of such discussions or negotiations (the “ Discussion Information ”).
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party and its Representatives shall not disclose to any person that any discussions or negotiations are taking place between the parties concerning a possible Transaction, including the content, timing and status of such discussions or negotiations (the “ Discussion Information ”).
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "Without the prior written consent of the Company or except as may be required by applicable law or regulation" creates a legal risk. This phrase protects the Receiving Party in case a disclosure is legally mandated (e.g., by a subpoena). By omitting this, the Receiving Party could be in breach of contract even if forced to disclose by law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text accounts for scenarios where disclosure is compelled by law, such as through a subpoena under FRCP 45. By removing the 'except as required by law' clause, the modified text puts the Receiving Party in a position where complying with a legal subpoena could constitute a breach of contract. FRCP 45 governs subpoenas, and failing to comply with a valid subpoena can result in legal penalties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text included an exception for disclosures required by law, protecting the Receiving Party if legally compelled to disclose information. The changed text removes this exception, potentially placing the Receiving Party in breach of contract if they comply with a legal mandate such as a subpoena. The Law Explanation highlights FRCP 45 and the potential for penalties for failing to comply with a valid subpoena. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Rule 45, which governs subpoenas. The omission of the 'except as required by law' clause directly contradicts the Receiving Party's ability to comply with legal obligations without breaching the contract.
